identity.title: No cancellation path on a subscription/account page

knownLimitations: Absence of a visible cancel path is not proof that cancellation is impossible.

catalogMetadata.sourceSummary: FTC staff report covering dark pattern taxonomy, preselection, difficult cancellation, countdowns, buried terms, junk fees, and choice design.

officialSourceReviewProvenance.sourceLocator: Bringing Dark Patterns to Light, Introduction p. 1 and Background pp. 2-3, difficult cancellation and maze-screen examples.
officialSourceReviewProvenance.mappingNote: The FTC staff report discusses interface practices that can distort consumers' understanding or ability to act; FairUX uses it as contextual support for a missing or hard-to-find cancellation path for an active subscription, not as a rule-specific compliance finding.
officialSourceReviewProvenance.limitations: FTC staff report provides enforcement and research context; it does not create a standalone legal finding.

source.title: Rule Concerning the Use of Prenotification Negative Option Plans

catalogMetadata.sourceSummary: Current 16 CFR Part 425 text for prenotification negative option plans after court-driven removal of the vacated 2024 amendments.

officialSourceReviewProvenance.sourceLocator: 16 CFR Part 425.1(a)(1)(iii), 425.1(a)(2)-(3), and FTC 2026 ANPRM pp. 4-5 summary of subscriber cancellation procedures for prenotification plans.
officialSourceReviewProvenance.mappingNote: Current Part 425 addresses disclosures and procedures for prenotification negative option plans; FairUX treats it as contextual support for a missing or hard-to-find cancellation path for an active subscription because the current rule does not cover most modern subscriptions.
officialSourceReviewProvenance.limitations: Current Part 425 is limited to prenotification negative option plans and must not be treated as direct support for modern subscriptions, free trials, or cancellation UX outside that scope.

officialSourceReviewProvenance.sourceLocator: Federal Register Negative Option Rule final amendments, Supplementary Information B.1 and former § 425.6 Simple cancellation.
officialSourceReviewProvenance.mappingNote: The vacated 2024 final rule record explains FTC reasoning about negative-option cancellation paths that are missing or hard to find; FairUX retains it only as historical agency rationale, not current regulatory authority.
officialSourceReviewProvenance.limitations: Vacated source is retained only as historical FTC rationale and must not be used as current rule text.

officialSourceReviewProvenance.limitations: ANPRM is proposed rulemaking and a request for comment, not a final rule, current requirement, or legal verdict.

runtimeOfficialSources.title: How do we manage consent in practice?

knownLimitations: Computed CSS, viewport position, color contrast, and responsive variants are outside the current static provider.

catalogMetadata.sourceSummary: Official EDPB guidance on consent specificity, active choice, granularity, and bundling.

officialSourceReviewProvenance.sourceLocator: Guidelines 05/2020, sections 3.1 Free/freely given, 3.1.1 Imbalance of power, and EEA reading note, pp. 3 and 7-10.
officialSourceReviewProvenance.mappingNote: EDPB consent guidance supports genuine, free choice and the ability to refuse without detriment across EU and EEA contexts; FairUX treats it as contextual because it does not directly prescribe button color, size, contrast, or visual prominence.
officialSourceReviewProvenance.limitations: Does not directly regulate visual styling such as contrast, size, or prominence; ICO guidance carries the direct prominence mapping for this rule.

source.title: How do we manage consent in practice?

catalogMetadata.sourceSummary: Official UK guidance for storage/access consent mechanisms, equal ease of refusal, positive opt-in action, granular options, and withdrawal.

officialSourceReviewProvenance.sourceLocator: ICO "How do we manage consent in practice?", At a glance bullets and "Our consent mechanism makes it as easy to refuse consent as it is to accept" checklist.
officialSourceReviewProvenance.mappingNote: ICO consent guidance gives operational expectations for equal refusal, positive opt-in, and granular controls; this directly supports FairUX review of accept and reject consent actions presented with unequal visual prominence in GB consent contexts.
officialSourceReviewProvenance.limitations: Consent source supports governance review only and does not determine legal compliance for any individual UI.

catalogMetadata.sourceSummary: FTC staff report covering dark pattern taxonomy, preselection, difficult cancellation, countdowns, buried terms, junk fees, and choice design.

officialSourceReviewProvenance.sourceLocator: Bringing Dark Patterns to Light, Section IV printed pp. 15-16 on highlighted preferred choices and greyed rejection, plus Appendix A p. 25 bold Accept/greyed Reject example.
officialSourceReviewProvenance.mappingNote: The FTC staff report discusses interface practices that can distort consumers' understanding or ability to act; FairUX uses it as contextual support for accept and reject consent actions presented with unequal visual prominence, not as a rule-specific compliance finding.
officialSourceReviewProvenance.limitations: FTC staff report provides enforcement and research context; it does not create a standalone legal finding.

positive.summary: Accept is styled as a primary button while reject is a weaker link when experimental rules are enabled.

negative.summary: The experimental visual-imbalance rule is excluded from default scans.

identity.title: Bundled consent in a single control

runtimeOfficialSources.title: How do we manage consent in practice?

knownLimitations: The rule cannot determine whether topics are legally separable in a specific jurisdiction.

catalogMetadata.sourceSummary: Official EDPB guidance on consent specificity, active choice, granularity, and bundling.

officialSourceReviewProvenance.sourceLocator: Guidelines 05/2020, section 3.1.3 Granularity, paragraphs 42-44, p. 12.
officialSourceReviewProvenance.mappingNote: EDPB consent guidance ties valid consent to freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous choice; this directly supports FairUX review of one consent action bundling multiple processing purposes that should be separable in EU and EEA consent contexts.
officialSourceReviewProvenance.limitations: Consent source supports governance review only and does not determine legal compliance for any individual UI.

officialSourceReviewProvenance.limitations: Consent source supports governance review only and does not determine legal compliance for any individual UI.

source.title: Planet49 judgment on cookie consent and pre-ticked boxes
source.publisher: Court of Justice of the European Union

catalogMetadata.sourceSummary: Primary EU case law on active cookie consent and pre-ticked checkbox consent.

officialSourceReviewProvenance.sourceLocator: Planet49 judgment, paragraphs 49-64 and operative part on pre-ticked cookie checkbox consent.
officialSourceReviewProvenance.mappingNote: Planet49 rejects pre-ticked cookie consent as valid consent; FairUX uses that case-law signal for preselected checkbox controls in a consent context while avoiding a verdict on each implementation.
officialSourceReviewProvenance.limitations: Consent source supports governance review only and does not determine legal compliance for any individual UI.

officialSourceReviewProvenance.mappingNote: EDPB consent guidance ties valid consent to freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous choice; this directly supports FairUX review of preselected checkbox controls in a consent context in EU and EEA consent contexts.
officialSourceReviewProvenance.limitations: Consent source supports governance review only and does not determine legal compliance for any individual UI.

catalogMetadata.sourceSummary: FTC guidance for total-price and fee-disclosure review in the sectors covered by the unfair or deceptive fees rule.

officialSourceReviewProvenance.sourceLocator: FTC Unfair or Deceptive Fees FAQ, "Which mandatory fees or charges must be included" and "When must a business disclose the final amount of payment" sections.
officialSourceReviewProvenance.mappingNote: The FTC fees FAQ explains total-price and payment-stage disclosure expectations; FairUX uses it to support review of checkout-adjacent pricing that lacks fee context before payment without declaring a fee unfair or deceptive.

source.title: Dark commercial patterns
source.publisher: Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development

catalogMetadata.sourceSummary: Intergovernmental policy paper defining dark commercial pattern taxonomy and review vocabulary.

officialSourceReviewProvenance.sourceLocator: OECD Dark commercial patterns report, taxonomy discussion of obstruction and forced action in Digital Economy Papers No. 336.
officialSourceReviewProvenance.mappingNote: The OECD taxonomy describes dark commercial pattern mechanisms that pressure consumer decisions; FairUX uses it as non-jurisdictional context for decline copy that pressures the user through guilt or social discomfort.
officialSourceReviewProvenance.limitations: Policy report provides taxonomy context only and is not jurisdiction-specific enforcement authority.

catalogMetadata.sourceSummary: FTC staff report covering dark pattern taxonomy, preselection, difficult cancellation, countdowns, buried terms, junk fees, and choice design.

officialSourceReviewProvenance.mappingNote: The FTC staff report discusses interface practices that can distort consumers' understanding or ability to act; FairUX uses it as contextual support for decline copy that pressures the user through guilt or social discomfort, not as a rule-specific compliance finding.
officialSourceReviewProvenance.limitations: FTC staff report provides enforcement and research context; it does not create a standalone legal finding.

runtimeOfficialSources.title: Unfair Commercial Practices Directive Annex I limited-time claims

knownLimitations: The rule detects pressure signals but cannot verify claim truthfulness.

source.title: Unfair Commercial Practices Directive Annex I limited-time claims

catalogMetadata.sourceSummary: Primary EU unfair commercial practices text covering limited-time and limited-availability claim review.

officialSourceReviewProvenance.sourceLocator: Directive 2005/29/EC, Article 5(5) and Annex I misleading commercial practice 7 on false limited-time availability claims.
officialSourceReviewProvenance.mappingNote: The UCPD Annex I entry identifies false limited-time availability claims as unfair in all circumstances; FairUX uses it to ground countdown timer urgency claims in purchase or conversion flows without deciding whether a specific stock or time limit is factual.
officialSourceReviewProvenance.limitations: UCPD Annex I covers false limited-time or limited-availability claims; FairUX can flag scarcity language but cannot determine whether the underlying time limit or availability claim is true.

catalogMetadata.sourceSummary: FTC staff report covering dark pattern taxonomy, preselection, difficult cancellation, countdowns, buried terms, junk fees, and choice design.

officialSourceReviewProvenance.mappingNote: The FTC staff report discusses interface practices that can distort consumers' understanding or ability to act; FairUX uses it as contextual support for countdown timer urgency claims in purchase or conversion flows, not as a rule-specific compliance finding.
officialSourceReviewProvenance.limitations: FTC staff report provides enforcement and research context; it does not create a standalone legal finding.

source.title: Negative Option Rule final rule amendments

catalogMetadata.sourceSummary: Historical FTC rulemaking record for the 2024 click-to-cancel amendments.
catalogMetadata.statusNote: The amended 2024 rule was vacated by the Eighth Circuit on 2025-07-08 and is retained only as historical agency reasoning, not current enforceable rule text.

officialSourceReviewProvenance.sourceLocator: Federal Register Negative Option Rule final amendments, Supplementary Information B.1 and former §§ 425.4-425.6 disclosures, consent, and cancellation.
officialSourceReviewProvenance.mappingNote: The vacated 2024 final rule record explains FTC reasoning about negative-option free-trial signup copy lacking renewal disclosure context; FairUX retains it only as historical agency rationale, not current regulatory authority.
officialSourceReviewProvenance.limitations: Vacated source is retained only as historical FTC rationale and must not be used as current rule text.

source.title: Negative Option Rule: Advance Notice of Proposed Rulemaking

catalogMetadata.sourceSummary: FTC 2026 ANPRM requesting comment on whether and how to amend the current Negative Option Rule after the 2024 amendments were vacated.
catalogMetadata.statusNote: The 2026 ANPRM requests public comment on possible amendments after the 2024 amendments were vacated; it is proposed rulemaking material, not final rule text or a current requirement.

officialSourceReviewProvenance.sourceLocator: Negative Option Rule 2026 ANPRM, pp. 3-4 free trial conversion examples and pp. 6-8 existing requirements discussion.
officialSourceReviewProvenance.mappingNote: The 2026 ANPRM asks whether negative-option requirements should be updated; FairUX uses it as proposed rulemaking context for free-trial signup copy lacking renewal disclosure context, not as a current obligation.
officialSourceReviewProvenance.limitations: ANPRM is proposed rulemaking and a request for comment, not a final rule, current requirement, or legal verdict.

positive.summary: Free-trial CTA without nearby renewal or billing-start disclosure is flagged.
positive.testCase: flags a free-trial CTA with no nearby renewal disclosure [en]

negative.summary: Free-trial CTA with nearby auto-renewal price and cancellation terms stays quiet.
negative.testCase: does not flag when renewal is disclosed nearby [negative]
